titel: Government subsidies for Swedish schools abroad – an outdated and inefficient system (Report 2026:11).
html: The Swedish National Audit Agency has reviewed the system of state subsidies for Swedish schools abroad and concludes that the system is outdated and inefficiently designed. The link between the subsidy and a clear Swedish societal interest is weaker today than when the system was introduced. The regulations allow for a large degree of interpretation, which makes it difficult to ensure a purposeful and legally sound process. At the same time, the supervisory, evaluation, and quality assurance functions of the school authorities are not functioning effectively in practice. Overall, the National Audit Agency assesses that the state's responsibility is unclear and unevenly distributed among different groups of students residing abroad. When the current system was established, the intention was to facilitate recruitment for employers who need to station personnel abroad, such as embassies, aid organizations, and multinational corporations. It was considered to be in the interest of Swedish society that these employers could send personnel to operations abroad. In the early 1990s, there were more than 30 Swedish schools abroad in Europe, Africa, Asia, and South America. Today, there are 14 schools, including 2 in Africa, 7 in Spain, and the rest in European capitals. The National Audit Agency's review shows that the majority of the parents of students who are eligible for state subsidies today are residing abroad on their own initiative, while only a smaller proportion belong to the groups that the system was originally intended for. Nearly half of these students have parents who are either employed by the school or are self-employed. Against this background, the link between the subsidy and a clear Swedish societal interest is weaker today than at the time the system was created. The National Audit Agency's assessment is that Regulation (1994:519) concerning state subsidies for the education of Swedish children and young people abroad allows for...
tldr: The Swedish National Audit Office has reviewed the funds that the Swedish government provides to schools abroad and has found that the system is outdated and flawed. It is no longer clear why the government should fund these schools, as most students do not belong to the groups that the system was originally intended to support.
lattlast:
The Swedish National Audit Office is an agency that monitors how the state uses its funds. They have investigated the subsidies provided to Swedish schools located in other countries and concluded that the system is outdated and inefficient. It is difficult to determine precisely how the rules should be applied, which makes it challenging to ensure that the money is used appropriately.

When the system was created, the goal was to help Swedish companies and organizations, such as embassies, to send personnel abroad. The idea was that it was beneficial for Sweden if these individuals could send their children to Swedish schools. Today, there are only 14 such schools remaining worldwide, primarily in Europe and Spain.

The audit reveals that most of the parents of students attending these schools have chosen to move abroad independently. They no longer represent the groups that the system was originally designed for, such as employees of Swedish government agencies. Nearly half of the parents are employed by the school itself or run their own businesses.

Because the recipients of the subsidies are no longer the groups that were originally intended, the Swedish National Audit Office believes that the connection to Sweden's interests is too weak. This makes it difficult to justify the state continuing to provide these funds in the same way as before.
